title: Local Models for Agentic Coding on Mac
subtitle: A friendly but substantial guide to MLX, llama.cpp, and Ollama on Apple silicon
description: A deeper beginner-friendly course on local coding models for Apple silicon Macs. It starts with plain-language mental models, then steadily builds toward runtimes, GGUF and quantization choices, local serving, tool calling, and realistic coding-agent workflows grounded in MLX, llama.cpp, Ollama, and respected local-coding guidance.

modules.title: Getting Oriented
modules.description: Understand what local models are, what agentic coding asks of them, and why Apple silicon changes the conversation.

lessons.title: What People Mean by Local Models
objectives: Understand local models as a deployment choice rather than a mysterious AI category.
See why coding workflows stress models differently from ordinary chat.
Build a simple mental map of privacy, latency, control, and hardware limits.
lessons.content:
When people say a model is local, the important part is not that it is small, open source, or magically private. The important part is where inference happens. In this course, local means the model runs on your own Apple silicon Mac instead of on someone else's server. That one change reshapes the whole experience: privacy is different, latency is different, cost is different, and the hardware ceiling is suddenly very personal.

That is why local models are best understood as a deployment choice. The same general family of model behaviors might exist in cloud and local settings, but the tradeoffs feel different. With local models, you are operating inside the memory, storage, and thermal comfort of one machine. That can feel empowering because everything is closer to you. It can also feel humbling because the machine's limits become impossible to ignore.

Why coding use cases are more demanding than chat

Ordinary chat asks for a response. Agentic coding asks for a chain of competent behavior. A coding assistant might inspect files, plan a change, call tools, read error output, revise the plan, and keep context across several turns. That is much harder than answering a single prompt well. It puts pressure on instruction following, context handling, tool use, and staying coherent over time.

This is why a local model that feels fun in a chat window can still feel weak inside a coding workflow. The bar is not just intelligence in the abstract. The bar is whether the model remains useful while doing work.

A practical starter lens

  • Privacy: some data can stay on-device.
  • Latency: local response can feel immediate when the model fits well.
  • Control: you choose the runtime, files, and serving layer.
  • Limits: your Mac's memory budget and runtime choice matter a lot.

If you hold those four ideas together, the whole local-model space gets less mystical. You are not entering a separate universe. You are making engineering tradeoffs on your own machine.
checklist: Write your own plain-language definition of a local model.
Write one sentence explaining why coding workflows are more demanding than basic chat.
List one reason local models appeal to you and one reason they still feel uncertain.
Name one task where privacy or control would make local inference feel especially valuable.
reflectionQuestions: If your Mac became a trustworthy little AI workshop tomorrow, what kind of coding help would feel genuinely useful instead of merely impressive?
Which part of the local-model story draws you in most right now: privacy, speed, control, curiosity, or the challenge of making it all work?

quiz.prompt: In this course, what makes a model local?
choices: The model always works offline with no setup.
It is automatically smaller than all cloud models.
Inference happens on your own Mac instead of a remote server.
It only answers programming questions.
quiz.explanation: Local refers to where the model runs, not to a fixed size or capability category.

quiz.prompt: Why does agentic coding ask more of a model than ordinary chat?
choices: Because it requires useful multi-step behavior with context and tools
Because it only uses code completion and nothing else
Because chat models never need memory
Because coding tasks remove the need for instruction following
quiz.explanation: Coding agents must stay coherent across inspection, tool use, revision, and follow-up work.

quiz.prompt: Which lens belongs in a healthy first mental model for local inference?
choices: Only benchmark scores matter.
Cloud and local deployments have identical tradeoffs.
If a model loads once, it is automatically practical.
Privacy, latency, control, and hardware limits all matter together.
quiz.explanation: Local workflows are shaped by several interacting tradeoffs, not one headline metric.

lessons.title: Why Apple Silicon Macs Stand Out
objectives: Understand why Apple silicon appears so often in local AI conversations.
Learn unified memory and platform coherence in plain language.
Evaluate whether a model merely runs on your Mac or actually feels good on your Mac.
lessons.content:
Apple silicon matters in this conversation because the hardware and software stack is unusually cohesive. MLX exists in part because Apple silicon offers a compelling environment for machine-learning workloads: unified memory, strong on-device performance characteristics, and a development story that makes local experimentation more pleasant than many people expect before they try it.

You do not need to become a chip expert to benefit from the core idea. The most practical concept is fit. A model, runtime, and workflow need to fit your machine well enough that the experience is usable. Apple silicon helps because the platform makes it relatively natural to exploit the hardware effectively, but it does not erase limits. You still have a finite memory budget. You still have tradeoffs between speed, size, and responsiveness.

In plain English: unified memory

MLX documentation emphasizes unified memory because it changes how people think about the machine. In a simplified sense, the system is designed so memory is shared in a way that is especially relevant to machine-learning workloads. For learners, the main takeaway is not the hardware diagram. It is that Apple silicon is not merely a regular laptop with a nice benchmark. It is a platform whose architecture makes local ML work feel unusually viable.

That said, the beginner trap is to treat viability as inevitability. A huge model can technically load and still feel miserable. A smaller or more heavily quantized model can feel dramatically better. Practical success comes from respecting the machine's comfort zone, not from forcing the biggest model that can barely start.

Comfort beats bragging rights

A useful local setup feels calm. It responds in a timeframe you can tolerate. It does not constantly put memory pressure on the rest of the system. It leaves you feeling in control rather than in a benchmark competition. Once you adopt that mindset, model selection becomes much healthier.
checklist: Explain unified memory in your own words without trying to sound overly technical.
Write down what 'comfortable to use' means for you in a local coding workflow.
List one reason Apple silicon is appealing for local models and one limit it does not remove.
Describe a situation where a smaller model might be more useful than a larger one.
reflectionQuestions: Imagine the smoothest local-model experience you would realistically want on your Mac. What would make it feel calm and trustworthy rather than merely possible?
If two setups gave equally good answers but one felt lighter, cooler, and more responsive, how much would that change your idea of what 'best' means?

quiz.prompt: Why does Apple silicon matter so much in local-model discussions?
choices: Because only Apple devices can run local models
Because the platform is unusually cohesive for local ML workloads
Because it removes the need for model selection
Because it guarantees every model will feel fast
quiz.explanation: The advantage is platform fit and practical usability, not exclusivity or magic.

quiz.prompt: What is the healthiest way to think about unified memory as a beginner?
choices: As proof that every large model will run well
As a reason to ignore runtime differences
As a replacement for understanding memory budgets
As a clue that Apple silicon is built in a way that can make local ML especially practical
quiz.explanation: Unified memory matters, but it does not cancel the need to choose models and runtimes carefully.

quiz.prompt: What is a stronger goal than making the biggest model barely launch?
choices: Choosing a setup that feels genuinely usable for daily work
Keeping Activity Monitor permanently maxed out
Avoiding all quantized models forever
Benchmarking even if the workflow feels miserable
quiz.explanation: Practical comfort is a better north star than parameter-count bragging rights.

modules.title: Meet the Tooling
modules.description: Learn the different jobs of MLX, llama.cpp, Ollama, and the model formats they rely on.

lessons.title: The Three Names You Will See Everywhere: MLX, llama.cpp, and Ollama
objectives: Separate platform, runtime, and serving-layer responsibilities mentally.
Understand why these tools are complementary rather than interchangeable.
Build a stack model that makes later setup decisions easier.
lessons.content:
One reason this topic feels confusing at first is that multiple layers get discussed as if they were one thing. MLX, llama.cpp, Ollama, GGUF, tool calling, serving, coding agents. If you do not separate the layers, the whole space becomes a soup of names. The fastest way to get unstuck is to ask, 'What job does this tool perform in the stack?'

MLX is closely tied to the Apple silicon mental model. It is not just a random library in the ecosystem. It helps explain why the platform is interesting and how machine-learning workloads can be expressed effectively on Apple hardware. For some learners, MLX is most valuable as a conceptual anchor: it makes the Apple-silicon story concrete.

llama.cpp is the practical runtime powerhouse in this space. Its README is dense but important because it touches several realities at once: efficient local inference, GGUF support, Apple Metal acceleration, quantized models, and even server modes that expose an OpenAI-compatible interface. If you want to understand why local model files and runtimes matter so much, llama.cpp is a central piece of the puzzle.

Ollama sits closer to the usability and integration layer. It makes local serving easier, gives other tools a predictable way to talk to models, and lowers the friction between 'I downloaded a model' and 'I can now use this through an API-like interface.' That convenience is a huge deal in practice.

A useful stack metaphor

  • MLX: helps you understand the Apple-silicon stage and native ML story
  • llama.cpp: helps run many local models efficiently, especially GGUF-style workflows
  • Ollama: helps serve and integrate those models into usable local workflows

This metaphor is imperfect, but it is good enough to reduce confusion. The win is not choosing one team mascot. The win is understanding the layer each tool occupies.
checklist: Write one sentence for MLX, one for llama.cpp, and one for Ollama.
Describe how a runtime differs from a serving layer.
Explain why the question 'Which one wins?' is less useful than 'Which job does it do?'
Sketch the local stack on paper or in your notes.
reflectionQuestions: Which of these three tools feels like the anchor point for your understanding so far, and which still feels like a blurry silhouette?
If you had to explain the local-model stack to a smart friend in under a minute, what metaphor would make the layers click?

quiz.prompt: What is the main advantage of separating MLX, llama.cpp, and Ollama mentally?
choices: It proves only one of them matters long term
It guarantees installation will be easy
It clarifies which layer each tool handles in the local stack
It means model formats no longer matter
quiz.explanation: Layer clarity is what keeps the ecosystem from feeling like one undifferentiated blur.

quiz.prompt: How is llama.cpp best understood in this course?
choices: As a practical local inference runtime with strong GGUF and Apple acceleration support
As Apple's official hardware abstraction layer
As a hosted cloud inference API
As a GUI note-taking app for prompts
quiz.explanation: llama.cpp sits at the runtime layer and is central to many local-model workflows.

quiz.prompt: What is one of Ollama's most important roles?
choices: Replacing all runtimes completely
Defining the GGUF specification
Handling only training, not inference
Serving models locally through a convenient interface other tools can call
quiz.explanation: Ollama lowers friction between local models and usable local applications.

quiz.prompt: Why does MLX matter even when you are not writing low-level code with it?
choices: It is required for every Ollama installation
It helps explain why Apple silicon is a serious local ML platform
It guarantees that all models will fit in memory
It removes the need to understand runtimes
quiz.explanation: MLX is both a tool and a conceptual guide to the Apple-silicon-local-ML story.

lessons.title: What GGUF, Quantization, and Context Really Mean
objectives: Understand GGUF as a packaging format in the local inference world.
Learn quantization as a practical tradeoff, not a buzzword.
Connect model size, context length, and workflow quality in a realistic way.
lessons.content:
As soon as you leave the beginner surface of local models, you run into terms like GGUF, quantization, and context length. These words can sound intimidating, but they become much more approachable when you keep asking what problem each one is solving.

GGUF matters because local runtimes need a practical way to package model weights and related metadata. In the llama.cpp world, GGUF is one of the central formats you will see. You do not need to memorize the file internals to benefit from the concept. The useful takeaway is that local inference depends on formats that make loading, running, and describing models practical for tools.

Quantization is about tradeoff. A full-precision model can be too large or too slow for comfortable local use. Quantized versions reduce resource demands by representing the model more compactly. This can improve speed and fit at the cost of some quality. The right beginner question is not, 'Is quantization bad?' The right question is, 'Does this quantized model still perform well enough for the task I care about on the machine I actually own?'

Context length adds another layer. Coding agents often need more than short conversational memory. They may need to keep track of many files, tool results, or multi-step plans. A model that feels fine in short chat can struggle badly when the workflow demands larger context. This is one reason respected coding-agent guides keep warning that a pleasant local chat model is not automatically a good coding-agent model.

Fit beats abstract maximums

  • GGUF: packaging that helps local runtimes handle models effectively
  • Quantization: a resource-versus-quality tradeoff
  • Context length: how much working text the system can effectively use during the task

Together, these concepts explain why local-model selection is never just about brand names. You are choosing a file format, a runtime fit, a memory profile, and a workflow experience.
checklist: Write a plain-language definition of GGUF.
Write a plain-language definition of quantization.
Explain why context needs may be higher for coding agents than for casual chat.
Describe one task where a smaller faster model might beat a larger slower model in real usefulness.
reflectionQuestions: If your local setup were a backpack for a long hike, what would count as packing smart rather than packing the biggest object you can physically shove inside?
When you imagine your own workflow, which matters more to you right now: longer context, faster iteration, lower friction, or higher single-answer quality?

quiz.prompt: Why does GGUF matter in many local workflows?
choices: It is a special Apple-only networking protocol.
It is a practical model packaging format used by important local runtimes.
It is the command-line tool that installs Ollama.
It is a replacement for quantization.
quiz.explanation: GGUF matters because it helps package models for efficient local handling.

quiz.prompt: What is the healthiest beginner view of quantization?
choices: It is a tradeoff between resource demands and useful quality.
It is always harmful and should be avoided.
It only affects training, not inference.
It guarantees a model will fit any Mac.
quiz.explanation: Quantization should be evaluated in relation to the actual task and machine.

quiz.prompt: Why can coding workflows expose model weaknesses that chat does not?
choices: Because coding never uses context
Because code is always easier than natural language
Because local APIs remove the need for reasoning
Because agentic tasks often need stronger context handling and multi-step consistency
quiz.explanation: Coding assistants place more sustained pressure on context, instruction following, and tool use.

quiz.prompt: What is a stronger model-selection principle than chasing the biggest file?
choices: Assume larger always means more productive
Avoid all model formats except one
Choose the setup that fits your machine and remains useful for your workflow
Ignore context-window needs until the end
quiz.explanation: Practical fit matters more than abstract maximum size.

modules.title: From Chat to Tools
modules.description: Move from talking to a local model toward serving it, calling tools, and integrating it into real work.

lessons.title: Why Local Serving Matters
objectives: Understand why local APIs are the bridge between chat experiments and real workflows.
See what Ollama contributes beyond simply running a model once.
Think of a local model as a service, not just a conversation window.
lessons.content:
There is a profound difference between 'I can chat with a model on my Mac' and 'my tools can reliably call a model on my Mac.' The first is a demo. The second is infrastructure. Once a model is served through a stable local interface, it becomes part of a workflow rather than a novelty tucked inside a single app.

Ollama is important here because its documentation makes the serving layer concrete. It helps turn local model execution into a predictable local endpoint. That means editors, scripts, coding assistants, and experiments can all speak to the same service rather than each inventing a separate ad hoc arrangement. This is one of the biggest jumps in practical usefulness.

A service mindset also changes how you think about reliability. If a model is part of your local toolchain, you care about whether it starts predictably, how you select the model, how the API behaves, and whether another tool can reuse the setup. Those are engineering questions, not just curiosity questions.

From interface to infrastructure

Imagine two local-model setups. One only works in a chat window and requires you to click around manually each time. The other exposes a local endpoint that multiple tools can call consistently. The second setup is much closer to how agentic coding becomes realistic, because tools can now compose around the model instead of depending on one UI.

Serving does not automatically make the model smarter. What it does is make the model more reachable. Reachability is what allows the rest of the local AI ecosystem to connect.
checklist: Write one sentence explaining the difference between chatting with a model and serving a model.
Name one tool you already use that could benefit from a local model endpoint.
Describe what becomes possible once multiple tools can call the same local model service.
List one way the service mindset changes how you think about reliability.
reflectionQuestions: If your Mac could quietly host one local model service for your daily work, what would you want that service to make easier without demanding constant babysitting?
Which feels more exciting to you right now: having a private chat companion or having a reusable local capability other tools can plug into?

quiz.prompt: What is the key shift when a local model is served through a stable interface?
choices: The model automatically gains larger context length
The model no longer needs storage space
The model becomes cloud-hosted
The model becomes something other tools can call as part of a workflow
quiz.explanation: Serving turns isolated usage into reusable local infrastructure.

quiz.prompt: Why does Ollama matter so much in this step?
choices: It defines the MLX tensor API
It removes all model-quality differences
It helps expose local models through a predictable, usable local interface
It replaces the need for runtimes
quiz.explanation: Ollama reduces the friction between a local model and a usable local service.

quiz.prompt: What does a service mindset add to local-model thinking?
choices: A guarantee that the model is good at coding
Questions about reliability, repeatability, and tool integration
An excuse to ignore setup details
Freedom from choosing models at all
quiz.explanation: Once a model becomes infrastructure, engineering concerns become part of the story.

lessons.title: How Tool Calling Turns into Agent Behavior
objectives: Understand tool calling as the practical bridge from chat to agent loops.
Learn why structured tool use matters for coding workflows.
See how local serving, tool calling, and iterative control form an agent stack.
lessons.content:
Tool calling is one of the clearest ways to understand agent behavior without drowning in hype. A model receives a task, determines that it needs outside information or an action, emits a structured request for a tool, receives the result, and continues reasoning from there. That loop is dramatically more powerful than raw chat because it lets the model interact with the world rather than only talk about it.

Ollama's tool-calling documentation matters because it makes this pattern explicit. The model is not just generating free-form text. It can request structured function calls with arguments that another layer executes. In a coding workflow, those tools might read files, search a repository, run tests, or inspect command output. Even when the model is weaker than a frontier cloud model, this pattern can make it substantially more useful than plain chat.

But tool calling also exposes weaknesses. The model has to choose the right tool, pass useful arguments, interpret tool output, and stay on task across multiple steps. This is why local coding agents can feel brittle with smaller or weaker models. The workflow raises the cognitive load.

Agent behavior as a loop

1. Receive the task
2. Inspect the available context
3. Decide whether a tool is needed
4. Call the tool with structured arguments
5. Read the result
6. Update the plan
7. Repeat until the work is complete or blocked

This is more than a prompt trick. It is a workflow shape. The more faithfully a local model can stay coherent inside that shape, the more 'agentic' it feels in practice.

A helpful mindset is to see tool calling as leverage, not magic. It can compensate for some weaknesses by grounding the model in real information, but it cannot make a fundamentally weak reasoning model behave like a much stronger one forever. The tool loop improves usefulness; it does not abolish model limits.
checklist: Write a plain-language definition of tool calling.
Describe one coding task that becomes far more useful when a model can call tools.
List the steps in a simple agent loop from task to tool use to revision.
Write down one reason tool calling helps and one reason it still fails with weaker models.
reflectionQuestions: Which part of the agent loop feels most magical to you right now, and which part feels most likely to break in a frustratingly human way?
If you could grant a local coding model one tool that would make it meaningfully more useful tomorrow, what would that tool be and why?

quiz.prompt: What does tool calling let a model do that plain chat does not?
choices: Interact with external tools through structured requests
Remove the need for any runtime or API
Guarantee perfect reasoning
Fit unlimited context into memory
quiz.explanation: Tool calling lets the model act through a workflow instead of only responding in prose.

quiz.prompt: Why can tool calling make local models more useful for coding?
choices: It turns every weak model into a frontier model
It removes the need for planning
It bypasses all context limits
It gives the model access to grounded information such as files and command output
quiz.explanation: Grounded tools can help the model operate on real project state rather than guessing.

quiz.prompt: What remains difficult even with tool calling?
choices: Returning any text output at all
Choosing appropriate tools and staying coherent across multiple steps
Running on macOS
Talking to a local API
quiz.explanation: Multi-step coherence and judgment remain core bottlenecks for smaller local models.

quiz.prompt: What is the healthiest way to think about tool calling?
choices: As a magical fix for all model limitations
As something only cloud models can do
As leverage that improves usefulness without erasing model limits
As an optional novelty unrelated to coding workflows
quiz.explanation: Tool calling is powerful, but it still depends on the underlying model's abilities.

modules.title: Real-World Expectations
modules.description: Learn where local coding models shine, where they still struggle, and how to build a sane personal workflow.

lessons.title: Where Local Coding Models Shine and Where They Still Struggle
objectives: Identify the coding tasks that local models handle best today.
Recognize common failure modes in reasoning, planning, and long-context work.
Develop realistic expectations that prevent frustration.
lessons.content:
Local coding models can be genuinely useful, but they are not useful in every way at the same level. They often shine in private code exploration, quick file-level edits, structured refactoring suggestions, documentation drafting, local note summarization, and lightweight automation loops where the toolchain can ground them. They become less dependable as planning depth, context size, subtle reasoning, and multi-step correction burdens increase.

This is why respected practical guides like Aider's local-model documentation are so valuable. They do not simply say 'local is good' or 'local is bad.' They point out where smaller models tend to break down: insufficient context, brittle instruction following, weak long-horizon planning, or tool misuse. That is the kind of honesty that helps you build satisfying workflows instead of disappointing ones.

A mature local-model user gets good at matching tasks to model strength. Maybe a local model is great for exploring a repository structure, drafting commit messages, or proposing small edits privately. Maybe a larger cloud model is still your choice for highly complex cross-file architectural work. That is not failure. It is workflow design.

Good fits and weak fits

  • Good fits: local note summarization, focused code explanation, small edits, pattern search, private experimentation
  • Mixed fits: multi-file refactors, long debugging chains, broader project planning
  • Weak fits: very long-context synthesis, subtle high-stakes architectural judgment, agent loops that demand many flawless steps

The real skill is not cheering for local models as a category. It is building a clear sense of where local capability ends and where another tool becomes the wiser choice.
checklist: List three coding tasks where a local model might be a strong fit for you.
List two tasks where you would still be cautious about relying on a local model.
Describe one common failure mode you now expect rather than taking personally.
Write a short rule of thumb for matching task complexity to model capability.
reflectionQuestions: Which coding tasks in your life feel small enough to benefit from local privacy and speed, yet meaningful enough that the setup would actually earn its keep?

Where do you think your own frustration threshold sits: are you more willing to tolerate weaker answers for privacy and control, or do you want local models to prove themselves on genuinely hard work?

quiz.prompt: What is a healthy way to evaluate local coding models?
choices: Assume any local model should replace stronger cloud tools immediately
Judge them only by single-turn chat impressiveness
Match tasks to model strengths and limitations instead of expecting universal excellence
Ignore context demands entirely
quiz.explanation: Useful local workflows come from task-model matching, not from category loyalty.

quiz.prompt: Which task is often a weaker fit for many local models?
choices: A long, subtle, multi-step architectural reasoning task
A short private code explanation
A small localized edit
A quick repository summary
quiz.explanation: Long-horizon complex reasoning remains one of the harder areas for smaller local models.

quiz.prompt: Why are guides like Aider's local-model documentation valuable?
choices: They guarantee all Ollama models perform equally well
They give grounded advice about where local coding workflows hold up and where they break
They replace the need to test your own workflow
They are official Apple hardware references
quiz.explanation: Practical guidance helps set realistic expectations and avoid cargo-cult setup choices.

lessons.title: A Good Beginner Mindset for Local Agents
objectives: Build a sensible first local-agent workflow on Mac without turning it into a giant infrastructure project.
Adopt evaluation habits that help you improve the setup over time.
Leave the course with a realistic, motivating next step.
lessons.content:
The best beginner mistake to avoid is turning local AI into an identity project. You do not need a maximal stack on day one. You need one workflow that is useful enough to teach you something. That might be a local coding assistant for small repo tasks, a local summarizer for private notes, or a model endpoint you can experiment with from the command line and an editor. The right first win is a repeated win.

A strong beginner workflow on Apple silicon usually has four properties. First, it is small enough to run comfortably. Second, it uses a serving layer you understand. Third, it keeps the task scope honest. Fourth, it gives you feedback about whether the model is actually helping. If you build around those principles, you are much less likely to burn out on setup theater.

A simple evaluation loop

  1. Choose one model and one runtime path.
  2. Run a short list of real tasks you actually care about.
  3. Note where the model feels fast, slow, useful, weak, or fragile.
  4. Change one variable at a time: model, quantization, context setting, or workflow.
  5. Keep the setup that improves your real work, not your benchmark pride.

This is how local-model skill grows. Not from reading discourse forever, but from small honest experiments with your own workflow.

The encouraging truth is that local models do not have to beat frontier systems at everything to be worthwhile. They only need to earn a place in your process. A private, controllable, good-enough tool that reliably helps with certain tasks can be a real asset on a Mac. That is a grounded, hopeful ending because it turns the question from 'Can local models do everything?' into 'What can they do well enough for me to want them around?'
checklist: Choose one small local-agent workflow you actually want to try first.
Write a short evaluation loop with the real tasks you will use to judge it.
Decide which variable you would change first if the workflow feels weak.
Write one sentence defining success for your first local-agent experiment.
reflectionQuestions: What would make your first local-agent experiment feel like a satisfying beginning rather than an exhausting science fair project?
If local models never replaced your best cloud tools but became a quietly useful part of your coding life, what role would you most want them to play?

quiz.prompt: What is the strongest mindset for a beginner local-agent workflow?
choices: Benchmark every model before defining a task
Assume bigger is better even if the experience is clumsy
Delay all experimentation until you fully understand the whole ecosystem
Start with one useful repeated workflow instead of a giant maximal setup
quiz.explanation: Small repeated wins teach more than elaborate local-AI theater.

quiz.prompt: What makes an evaluation loop useful?
choices: It focuses only on token throughput screenshots
It compares real tasks and changes one variable at a time
It avoids noting failure because that feels discouraging
It changes many settings at once for speed
quiz.explanation: Honest task-based evaluation tells you whether the setup is helping your actual work.

quiz.prompt: When does a local model earn a place in your workflow?
choices: Only if it surpasses the strongest cloud model on every task
Only if it runs completely offline forever
When it is useful enough on real tasks that you want to keep using it
When it has the largest parameter count you can find
quiz.explanation: Usefulness, not total dominance, is the right test for a personal workflow.

quiz.prompt: Which beginner habit most reduces setup theater?
choices: Starting from task value and adjusting the setup with small honest experiments
Changing many variables at once to find the perfect setup quickly
Assuming every local tool must be installed immediately
Treating model choice as a permanent identity statement
quiz.explanation: Task-first experimentation is what keeps the learning process grounded.
